title: Add cache-aside pattern for popular products
description: Implements a cache-aside strategy for the most popular products page to reduce database load during traffic spikes.
diff:
diff --git a/services/popular_products.py b/services/popular_products.py
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..c3d4e5f
--- /dev/null
+++ b/services/popular_products.py
@@ -0,0 +1,28 @@
+from __future__ import annotations
+import json
+from redis import Redis
+from database import get_session
+
+CACHE_KEY = "popular_products"
+CACHE_TTL = 60  # 1 minute
+
+def get_popular_products(redis: Redis) -> list[dict]:
+    cached = redis.get(CACHE_KEY)
+    if cached:
+        return json.loads(cached)
+
+    # Cache miss — fetch from database
+    # Under high traffic, ALL concurrent requests will miss cache simultaneously
+    # and flood the database with identical queries (cache stampede)
+    session = get_session()
+    products = session.execute(
+        "SELECT * FROM products ORDER BY view_count DESC LIMIT 50"
+    ).fetchall()
+    
+    result = [{"id": p.id, "name": p.name, "views": p.view_count} for p in products]
+    redis.setex(CACHE_KEY, CACHE_TTL, json.dumps(result))
+    return result
comments: oscar: Simple and effective caching. Approve.

pat: This is vulnerable to cache stampede. When the cache expires, all concurrent requests see the miss and hit the database simultaneously. During a flash sale with 10K RPS, this would generate 10K identical DB queries at once.
quinn: Use a lock-based recompute pattern or stale-while-revalidate. Only one request should rebuild the cache while others serve slightly stale data.
sre-rachel: We saw this exact pattern take down the product DB last Black Friday.
author-sam: I'll add a mutex for cache recomputation.
author: sam
